informal: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes such that $p-1$ divides $q-1$. If $(n, p q)=1$, show that $n^{q-1} \equiv 1(p q)$.
lean:
theorem Ireland_Rosen_exercise_3_14 {p q n : ℕ} (hp0 : p.Prime ∧ p > 2)
  (hq0 : q.Prime ∧ q > 2) (hpq0 : p ≠ q) (hpq1 : p - 1 ∣ q - 1)
  (hn : n.gcd (p*q) = 1) :
  n^(q-1) ≡ 1 [MOD p*q]
reasoning: The Lean signature captures all the components of the informal statement: p and q are primes greater than 2 (i.e., odd primes), they are distinct (p ≠ q), p-1 divides q-1, and n is coprime to pq (encoded as gcd(n, p*q) = 1). The conclusion n^(q-1) ≡ 1 [MOD p*q] matches "n^{q-1} ≡ 1 (mod pq)". Working in ℕ is appropriate here since gcd and modular congruence are well-defined, and the statement is equivalent to the informal claim. No hidden hypotheses are added or omitted.
